findings.description: `_new_token` uses `random.choice` (Mersenne Twister), a non-CSPRNG; session tokens are security-sensitive material and a predictable PRNG allows token-forgery attacks — use `secrets.choice` or `secrets.token_urlsafe` instead.

`create()` unconditionally overwrites an existing entry if `_new_token()` returns a colliding token, silently replacing one user's live session with another's — no uniqueness check or retry loop is present.
`create()` never consults `user_sessions()` or enforces any per-user session cap, allowing a single user to accumulate an unbounded number of sessions and exhaust memory.

`touch()` calls `self._sessions.get(token)` directly without invoking `_purge_expired()` first, so an already-expired-but-not-yet-purged session can be successfully renewed, resurrecting a session that should be dead.
`user_sessions()` iterates `self._sessions.values()` without calling `_purge_expired()`, so it returns sessions whose TTL has elapsed, giving callers a stale view of active sessions.

No locking protects any method; in a multi-threaded deployment the compound read-modify-delete in `_purge_expired()` and the read-then-write in `create()` / `touch()` are non-atomic, creating TOCTOU race conditions that can corrupt the session map or silently drop sessions.

The `ttl` constructor parameter accepts any integer including zero or negative values; a caller passing `ttl=0` creates sessions that are immediately expired, silently failing with no error or log entry.
